planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep and 6–8 inches apart after the last frost in well-drained, sandy, moderately fertile soil in full sun; allow bulbs to dry completely before each winter in zones 7 and colder by lifting and storing in a cool, dry place.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep after the last frost in well-drained, sandy soil in full sun; the Aztec lily (Jacobean lily) produces one of the most exotic and striking flowers in the bulb world — a deep, velvety crimson bloom resembling a Tudor rose or an orchid, with three upward-pointing petals and three downward-curving petals surrounding yellow-tipped stamens, emerging on a leafless stem before or with the strap leaves. Each bulb produces 1–2 flowers in spring. After bloom, allow the foliage to ripen completely before cutting it back. In zones 8–11, leave bulbs in the ground year-round in well-drained soil; in colder zones dig and store dry over winter at 50–60°F.

planting.note: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in zones 8–10 in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; in colder zones plant in spring after last frost and lift corms before fall frost. Sparaxis requires a dry summer dormancy.

directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, sandy soil in full sun in mild-winter zones; harlequin flower is a South African corm-grown plant that produces strikingly colorful, 6-petaled star-shaped flowers in vivid combinations of orange, red, purple, pink, white, and yellow with contrasting dark eyes and yellow centers from mid-spring to early summer. In zones 8–10 with dry summers (Mediterranean climate), corms can stay in the ground year-round; elsewhere dig and store dry over summer. Plant in generous drifts of 25–50 corms for the best effect — individual plants have little visual impact. Excellent in rock gardens, sunny borders, and containers. Naturalize readily in warm, dry California-style climates.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep and 3–4 inches apart as early as possible in fall in moist but well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade; fritillaria bulbs dehydrate quickly — plant immediately upon receipt. Naturalize in moist meadows or under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep as early in fall as possible in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade; snake's head fritillary is one of the most elegant spring bulbs, producing nodding, bell-shaped flowers with a distinctive tessellated (checkered) pattern of deep purple and pale lilac — an appearance so unique it earned the name 'snakeshead.' Plant in large drifts of 50 or more for visual impact in moist meadow grass or under deciduous trees where the grass provides support for the delicate stems. Allow the foliage to die down naturally after bloom. Do not allow the soil to dry out in summer — unlike most bulbs, fritillarias prefer consistently moist conditions. Plants slowly naturalize in ideal conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Siberian squill naturalizes by self-seeding and offsets to form large, sweeping colonies under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant small bulbs 3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun under deciduous trees; Siberian squill is one of the most intense true-blue flowers in the spring garden, producing small nodding bells of vivid cobalt-blue in earliest spring, often emerging through late snow. It is one of the best naturalizing bulbs for shaded areas, spreading slowly by both seed and offsets to carpet woodland floors with color by late March. After bloom the foliage yellows and disappears by late spring, leaving no trace. Plant in drifts of 100 or more under deciduous trees or in lawn areas for the most striking seasonal display. They are not harmed by mowing once the foliage has yellowed.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in zones 8–10 in well-drained, sandy, slightly acid soil in full sun; in colder zones plant in spring after last frost and lift after foliage dies. Ixia requires a warm, dry summer dormancy to bloom well.

directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in sharply drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; corn lily produces slender, wiry, grass-like stems bearing clusters of star-shaped flowers in luminous shades of white, yellow, pink, orange, red, and blue-green with contrasting dark eyes in late spring. Individual plants are airy and delicate — plant in generous clusters of 25 or more for maximum visual effect. The flowers close in cloudy weather and open fully only in bright sunlight. After bloom, allow foliage to die down completely before removing it; in climates with wet summers, lift corms and store dry until fall. Naturalize readily in well-drained, dry-summer California gardens. Excellent cut flower with long vase life.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 1–2 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; crimson flag lily thrives in moist spots alongside ponds and streams and blooms in fall when few other bulbs are in flower.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade near pond margins, stream banks, or in rain garden edges; crimson flag lily (formerly Schizostylis coccinea) is a South African plant that produces brilliant scarlet or deep pink star-shaped flowers on slender stems in autumn — September through November — at a time when most flowering perennials are winding down. The grassy, strap-like leaves are evergreen in mild climates. Plants require consistently moist soil throughout the growing season and will not perform well in dry conditions; they thrive where soil is reliably moist in summer. Clumps increase fairly quickly; divide every 3 years in spring to maintain vigor and control spread. 'Major' (large red) and 'Mrs. Hegarty' (pink) are popular cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in fall in well-drained, average to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; spring starflower naturalizes aggressively by offsets and self-seeding to form large, dense colonies.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade; spring starflower produces clusters of grassy, garlic-scented leaves in autumn and winter (the foliage smells of onion when bruised) followed by solitary, star-shaped, pale violet-blue to white flowers in early to mid-spring that hover just above the foliage on short stems. The plant naturalizes eagerly by both offsets and self-sowing to form dense ground-covering colonies under shrubs and deciduous trees. It tolerates dry summer dormancy and clay soil better than most small bulbs. Plant in large drifts of 100 or more for maximum effect. Excellent for zones 8–9 in the Gulf South where many Dutch bulbs fail — ipheion succeeds where tulips and hyacinths often cannot.

planting.note: Plant corms 4 inches deep and 3–4 inches apart in fall in well-drained, sandy to gravelly, dry-summer soil in full sun; triplet lily requires a completely dry summer dormancy and is best suited to the Pacific Coast and Mediterranean-climate gardens.

directions: Plant corms 4 inches deep in fall in well-drained, sandy to gravelly soil that dries out completely in summer; triplet lily (Ithuriel's spear) is a native California bulb that produces elegant umbels of funnel-shaped violet-blue flowers on tall, wiry stems in late spring, after which the plant enters complete summer dormancy. It is specifically adapted to the Mediterranean climate of the Pacific Coast and the dry-summer interior Southwest and will rot in humid eastern summers if the soil stays moist in summer. Excellent naturalized in gravel gardens, dry slopes, and rock gardens. Plant in drifts of 25 or more corms for the best display; individual plants are too sparse to appreciate. 'Queen Fabiola' is the best-known cultivar with larger, more deeply colored flowers.
